questions.questionText: The enteric nervous system plexus primarily responsible for regulating GI motility is the:
questions.explanation: Auerbach's plexus controls motility, while Meissner's controls secretion and blood flow.

questions.questionText: Gastric acid (HCl) is secreted by which cells of the stomach?
questions.explanation: Parietal cells secrete HCl and intrinsic factor.

questions.questionText: Pepsinogen is secreted by which cells of the stomach?
questions.explanation: Chief cells secrete pepsinogen, the inactive precursor of the protease pepsin.

questions.questionText: Gastrin is primarily secreted by G cells located in the:
questions.explanation: G cells in the antrum secrete gastrin in response to amino acids and distention.

questions.questionText: Which hormone strongly stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion?
questions.explanation: I cells in the duodenum and jejunum secrete CCK in response to fats and proteins.

questions.questionText: Secretin is released in response to acid in the duodenum and causes:
questions.explanation: S cells release secretin to neutralize the acid chyme via pancreatic bicarbonate.

questions.questionText: Vitamin B12 is absorbed primarily in the:
questions.explanation: Vitamin B12-intrinsic factor complex is absorbed by specific receptors in the terminal ileum.

questions.questionText: Bile salts are essential for the digestion and absorption of:
questions.explanation: Bile salts emulsify dietary fats, increasing the surface area for pancreatic lipase.

questions.questionText: The primary location for the absorption of most nutrients is the:
questions.explanation: Most carbohydrate, protein, and lipid absorption occurs in the duodenum and jejunum.

questions.questionText: Iron is primarily absorbed in which part of the GI tract?
questions.explanation: Iron is absorbed in the duodenum (Fe2+ state).

questions.questionText: In GI Physiology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Physiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Physiology references for GI Physiology. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: GI Physiology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical GI Physiology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Physiology. Chapter: GI Physiology.
